content.text: Shared Controls
The Shared Responsibility Model has a third category beyond 'AWS owns this' and 'Customer owns this':
shared controls
— areas where
both

AWS and the customer must implement the same type of control for their respective scopes. The control category is the same; the scope of application differs. Neither party can satisfy the other's half. A gap on either side is a genuine security gap.
AWS's official documentation identifies four shared control categories:
patch management
configuration management
awareness and training
, and (less explicitly)
logging and monitoring
. Understanding where your half begins ensures you don't assume AWS handles something it doesn't.

attrs.alt: Shared controls diagram: AWS implements for infrastructure layer, Customer implements for application layer, both must act

content.text: Shared Control 1: Patch Management

Patch management is the archetypal shared control. AWS patches its infrastructure (hypervisor, managed service runtimes, storage systems, network devices). You patch what you deploy on top of that infrastructure. The division is clean — but the customer side is easily neglected.
AWS patches (without customer action):

The Nitro hypervisor firmware, the physical server BIOS, network device operating systems, the RDS database engine (minor versions, automatically; major versions at customer initiation), Lambda runtime patches (minor version updates), ECS Fargate host OS, and S3/DynamoDB internal software.
Customer patches:

Guest OS on EC2 instances (kernel, glibc, openssl, any installed package), application runtimes you install (Python, Node.js, Java JVM on self-managed EC2), web servers (Apache, Nginx), any database you run on EC2, and container images in ECR (you push updated images; ECR Enhanced Scanning notifies you of new CVEs against existing images).
The patch lag problem:
The most common customer-side failure is not patch
policy
failure — it is patch
lag

. A team knows it should patch but delays for fear of breaking running services. The gap between a CVE being published (the clock starting) and the patch being applied is the exposure window. AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager automates this: scan daily, apply in a maintenance window, reboot if needed.
Tracking what needs patching:

Amazon Inspector continuously monitors EC2 instances and container images for CVEs. Inspector scores findings by CVSS severity and EPSS (Exploit Prediction Scoring System) — the probability of exploitation in the next 30 days. A HIGH CVSS vulnerability with a low EPSS score is less urgent than a MEDIUM CVSS vulnerability with a high EPSS score. Inspector surfaces this prioritization automatically.

# Automated patch compliance report via SSM
aws ssm describe-instance-patch-states-for-patch-group \
  --patch-group "production" \
  --query 'InstancePatchStates[*].{ID:InstanceId,Missing:MissingCount,Failed:FailedCount,Pending:InstalledPendingRebootCount}' \
  --output table

# Find instances missing critical patches (severity: Critical or High)
aws ssm describe-instance-patches \
  --instance-id i-0abc123def \
  --filters "Key=State,Values=Missing" "Key=Severity,Values=Critical,High" \
  --output table
Shared Control 2: Configuration Management

Configuration management is the practice of ensuring systems are configured securely, consistently, and in accordance with a documented baseline — and that deviations are detected and remediated. Both AWS and customers maintain configuration management programs for their respective scopes.
AWS's configuration management:

AWS maintains a documented configuration baseline for its infrastructure components (servers, network devices, hypervisor). Changes to infrastructure configuration go through a change management process including peer review, testing, and staged rollout. AWS publishes its SOC 2 Type II report annually, which includes the auditor's opinion on whether AWS's change management controls are effective — this is the evidence that AWS's half of configuration management is operating correctly.
Customer's configuration management:

You are responsible for defining and enforcing configuration baselines for every AWS resource you deploy: EC2 instance hardening standards, security group rule constraints, S3 bucket encryption requirements, RDS parameter group settings, Lambda function environment configuration, and IAM policy standards. These baselines should be codified in Infrastructure as Code (Terraform, CDK, CloudFormation) and enforced via policy scanning tools and AWS Config rules.
Configuration drift:

Drift occurs when the actual configuration of a resource diverges from its baseline — typically because someone made a manual change outside the IaC process. AWS Config continuously detects drift by comparing actual resource configurations to their recorded state. CloudFormation Stack Drift Detection identifies which resources in a stack have been changed outside CloudFormation. Treat drift findings as security events — unauthorized configuration changes may indicate an attacker trying to open access or disable controls.
AWS Config as the customer configuration management system:

AWS Config provides a time-series history of every configuration change to every AWS resource in your account. For compliance audits, you can pull the configuration history for any resource over any time range and show exactly how it was configured and when it changed. This replaces manual spreadsheet-based asset inventories with an automatically maintained, tamper-evident configuration database.

# AWS Config: list all configuration changes to a specific S3 bucket
aws configservice get-resource-config-history \
  --resource-type AWS::S3::Bucket \
  --resource-id my-production-bucket \
  --start-time 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time 2024-06-30T23:59:59Z \
  --query "configurationItems[*].{Time:configurationItemCaptureTime,Status:configurationItemStatus,Config:configuration}" \
  --output json | jq '.[0:5]'
Shared Control 3: Awareness and Training

Security awareness and training is explicitly listed by AWS as a shared control. AWS trains its own staff — security engineers, data center technicians, customer support personnel — on security procedures, social engineering resistance, and incident response. You must train your own employees and users.
AWS employee training:

AWS requires annual security awareness training for all employees with access to customer systems or data. Access to customer data (for support purposes) requires additional authorization and is logged. AWS SOC 2 reports include the auditor's assessment of AWS's training controls as evidence that this half of the shared control is operating. You do not need to implement this for AWS employees — AWS does.
Your organization's training responsibility:

You must ensure that everyone who uses AWS in your organization understands their security responsibilities: developers (secure coding, secrets management, IAM least privilege), operations (patch management, incident response), DevOps engineers (IaC security scanning, CI/CD pipeline security), executives (data classification policy, acceptable use).
Phishing and social engineering:

Cloud breaches often start with phishing — an employee clicks a malicious link, credentials are stolen, and the attacker logs into the AWS Console or calls the API with stolen access keys. Mandatory phishing simulation training (tools like KnowBe4, Proofpoint Security Awareness Training) reduces susceptibility. MFA stops phished passwords from being immediately useful, but training reduces the initial compromise rate.
AWS training resources for your team:

AWS provides extensive free security training: AWS Security Fundamentals (free digital course), AWS Security blog, AWS re:Inforce conference sessions on YouTube, AWS Well-Architected security review workshops, and AWS Skill Builder training paths. Investing in developer security training prevents vulnerabilities at the source, which is cheaper and more effective than detecting and remediating them post-deployment.

# Example security awareness training compliance tracking in AWS
# Use Systems Manager to tag instances with last-trained date for the admin
aws ec2 create-tags \
  --resources i-0abc123def456 \
  --tags Key=AdminTrainingDate,Value=2024-Q1 \
         Key=AdminTrainedBy,Value=security-team

# Better: enforce training via IAM condition
# Add a condition to sensitive actions requiring a specific session tag
# that is only present when federated through IdP after training completion
Shared Control 4: Logging and Monitoring

Logging and monitoring is a shared control where AWS and the customer each instrument their respective layers. AWS collects metrics, logs, and events from its infrastructure services. You configure and analyze the logs and metrics for your deployments.
AWS's logging layer:

AWS CloudTrail records API calls made to AWS services (the management plane). AWS generates service-level metrics in CloudWatch (e.g., EC2 CPU utilization, DynamoDB consumed capacity, RDS connections). AWS's internal systems generate infrastructure-level events that feed into GuardDuty's threat intelligence. These log streams are produced by AWS — but are delivered to your account for analysis.
Customer's logging configuration:

Receiving logs is not the same as having an effective monitoring program. You must: enable CloudTrail in all regions and deliver to a secured S3 bucket. Enable VPC Flow Logs for network visibility. Enable service-level logging (S3 server access logs, ALB access logs, RDS slow query log). Configure CloudWatch alarms for anomalous metrics. Enable GuardDuty and route findings to your SIEM or incident response system.
The monitoring gap:

AWS generates the raw data; building actionable detection on top of it is the customer's job. A common gap: CloudTrail is enabled (the logs exist) but nobody is alerting on high-severity events (root account login, IAM policy changes, security group modifications, S3 bucket policy changes). Without alerts, logs are only useful for post-breach forensics — valuable, but insufficient for real-time response.
Centralized log management:

For multi-account organizations, aggregate all CloudTrail, Config, VPC Flow Log, and GuardDuty findings into a central security account. Amazon Security Lake (built on S3 in OCSF format) or a SIEM (Splunk, Datadog, Elastic) provides cross-account visibility. A finding in one account that correlates with suspicious activity in another account only appears if logs are centralized.

# CloudWatch alarm: alert when root account is used
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "root_login" {
  alarm_name          = "root-account-usage"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 1
  metric_name         = "RootAccountUsage"
  namespace           = "CloudTrailMetrics"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = 1
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.security_alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}

# CloudWatch metric filter to generate the metric from CloudTrail logs
resource "aws_cloudwatch_log_metric_filter" "root_login" {
  name           = "RootAccountUsageFilter"
  pattern        = "{ $.userIdentity.type = \"Root\" && $.userIdentity.invokedBy NOT EXISTS && $.eventType != \"AwsServiceEvent\" }"
  log_group_name = aws_cloudwatch_log_group.cloudtrail.name

  metric_transformation {
    name      = "RootAccountUsage"
    namespace = "CloudTrailMetrics"
    value     = "1"
  }
}
How Shared Controls Appear in Compliance Frameworks

Compliance frameworks such as SOC 2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 include controls in each of these shared categories. When an auditor assesses your environment, they will evaluate both halves of shared controls: AWS's half (via AWS Artifact reports) and your half (via your own documentation and evidence).
Control Category
Framework Control Example
AWS Evidence
Customer Evidence
Patch Management
PCI 6.3: Protect all system components from known vulnerabilities by installing applicable security patches
AWS SOC 2 – CC7.1 (vulnerability management)
SSM Patch Compliance report, Inspector findings history
Configuration Management
SOC 2 CC6.1: Logical and physical access controls
AWS SOC 2 – CC6.1 through CC6.8
AWS Config compliance reports, Terraform IaC review evidence
Awareness & Training
ISO 27001 A.7.2.2: Information security awareness
AWS SOC 2 – CC1.4 (workforce training)
Training completion records, phishing simulation results
Logging & Monitoring
PCI 10.1: Implement audit trails to link all access to system components
CloudTrail generation confirmed in AWS SOC 2 CC7.2
CloudTrail enabled proof, log retention policy, SIEM alert rules
The key takeaway for compliance audits:
do not try to prove AWS's half yourself

. Download the relevant AWS Artifact report (SOC 2 Type II, PCI DSS AoC, ISO 27001 certificate) and reference it for the AWS-scope controls. Focus your documentation effort entirely on the customer-scope half — that is where auditors will ask for your specific evidence.
Responsibility Gaps: Where Teams Go Wrong
Shared controls are the most common source of compliance findings and security gaps because each party assumes the other handles it. Real examples of responsibility confusion:
'AWS handles patching' (false for EC2):

A common misconception. AWS patches the hypervisor; you patch the guest OS. An EC2 instance running Amazon Linux 2 with a six-month-old kernel is vulnerable to all CVEs published in that period — AWS will not apply those patches. Enable SSM Patch Manager with a 'scan and install' maintenance window.
'AWS monitors our logs' (false):

AWS generates CloudTrail events; you configure the alerting. Without CloudWatch alarms or a SIEM consuming CloudTrail, you have a log store but no monitoring. Enable GuardDuty (which does analyze logs automatically) and configure Security Hub findings to flow to your incident response system.
'Security training is HR's job' (partially true):

General security awareness is HR's domain. AWS-specific training — how to configure security groups, why not to commit access keys to Git, how to use Secrets Manager, what a GuardDuty finding means — is the engineering team's responsibility. AWS-specific security training is a shared control that requires a customer investment beyond generic annual awareness campaigns.
'Config management is just having Terraform' (insufficient):

Having IaC is necessary but not sufficient. You also need: static analysis scanning of IaC before apply (Checkov, tfsec), drift detection to catch manual changes, AWS Config rules to continuously assess deployed resources, and a change management process that prevents unreviewed changes from reaching production. IaC without these controls is a foundation that can be bypassed.
Building a Shared Controls Checklist
Use this checklist to verify both sides of each shared control are covered before declaring your AWS environment production-ready.
Shared Control
AWS Half (verify via Artifact)
Customer Half (verify yourself)
Patch Management
AWS SOC 2 CC7.1 covers AWS infrastructure patches
SSM Patch Manager enabled, Inspector active, patch SLA defined (e.g., Critical: 7d)
Configuration Management
AWS SOC 2 CC6.1–CC6.8 covers AWS config management
AWS Config enabled, Config rules defined, IaC enforced, drift detection active
Awareness & Training
AWS SOC 2 CC1.4 covers AWS staff training
Developer security training, phishing simulation, AWS-specific onboarding module
Logging & Monitoring
AWS SOC 2 CC7.2 covers AWS infrastructure logging
CloudTrail all-region, GuardDuty, Security Hub, VPC Flow Logs, CW alarms for critical events
Incident Response as a Shared Control

While AWS's official documentation lists four shared control categories, incident response functions as a de facto fifth. When a security incident occurs in your AWS environment, the response involves both parties — AWS handles incidents affecting its infrastructure, you handle incidents in your deployed resources. The boundary is important to understand before an incident occurs, not during one.
AWS-initiated responses:

If AWS detects abuse from your account (a compromised EC2 instance sending spam, a credential being used for unauthorized cryptocurrency mining), AWS may take protective action: rate-limiting outbound traffic, temporarily suspending specific API calls, or contacting you via the security email address on your account. These actions protect other AWS customers. You will receive an AWS abuse notification; responding to it is your responsibility.
Customer incident response for AWS resources:

When GuardDuty finds that an IAM role is being used from an unusual geographic location, or that an EC2 instance is communicating with a known command-and-control server, responding to that finding is entirely your responsibility. AWS does not automatically quarantine your resources, revoke your credentials, or investigate the incident. You need runbooks, an on-call rotation, and automation to respond.
AWS Support during incidents:

AWS Business and Enterprise Support customers can open a high-severity case and get a support engineer on the phone. Enterprise Support customers have access to a Technical Account Manager (TAM) and AWS Security for proactive guidance. AWS support engineers can help you understand which API calls were made and what service behavior is expected — but they cannot make changes in your account without your authorization and typically cannot access your data.
Preparing an incident response plan:

Before an incident occurs, document: who gets paged for different GuardDuty finding types, what automated response runs immediately (quarantine security group, revoke STS tokens, snapshot EBS volumes for forensics), and what the escalation path is. Test these runbooks with regular GameDay exercises where the security team simulates a breach scenario and the operations team responds.

# Lambda: respond to GuardDuty findings automatically via EventBridge
import boto3

ec2 = boto3.client("ec2")
iam = boto3.client("iam")

QUARANTINE_SG = "sg-0000quarantine"  # SG with no inbound/outbound

def handler(event, context):
    detail = event["detail"]
    finding_type = detail["type"]
    severity = detail["severity"]

    if severity >= 7 and "UnauthorizedAccess" in finding_type:
        # Revoke temporary IAM credentials
        role_name = detail["resource"]["accessKeyDetails"]["userName"]
        iam.put_role_policy(
            RoleName=role_name,
            PolicyName="EmergencyDenyAll",
            PolicyDocument='{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Deny","Action":"*","Resource":"*"}]}'
        )

    if "EC2" in finding_type and "instance" in detail.get("resource", {}):
        instance_id = detail["resource"]["instanceDetails"]["instanceId"]
        ec2.modify_instance_attribute(
            InstanceId=instance_id, Groups=[QUARANTINE_SG]
        )
Applying Shared Controls Across Multiple AWS Accounts

Operating multiple AWS accounts (development, staging, production, security tooling, log archive) multiplies the surface area where shared controls must be implemented. Each account requires its own instance of customer-half shared controls. AWS Organizations provides mechanisms to enforce shared control baselines consistently across all accounts.
Organization-level patch management policy:

Use AWS Systems Manager across your entire organization. Create an SSM Quick Setup host management configuration at the Organization level to automatically install and configure SSM Agent, enable patch management, and deploy CloudWatch Logs agents across all accounts and regions. New accounts automatically receive this baseline configuration when enrolled in Organizations.
Organization-wide configuration management:

Deploy AWS Config with an organization-wide aggregator to collect configuration data from all accounts into a central security account. Create AWS Config Conformance Packs — collections of Config rules — at the organization level, so every account automatically has the same configuration compliance rules without per-account setup.
Security training at scale:

For organizations with many AWS teams, build mandatory AWS security training into the onboarding process (training prerequisite for AWS Console access). Track completion in your identity provider and conditionally grant AWS access only after training is verified. This uses IAM Identity Center session attributes to inject a training-status claim into the federated session, which policies can then condition on.
Centralized monitoring baseline:

Deploy GuardDuty organization-wide with a single API call from the management account. All member accounts automatically have GuardDuty enabled with findings aggregating to the designated security account. Similarly, deploy Security Hub organization-wide and configure the AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standard across all accounts — giving you a standardized security score across the entire organization from day one.

# Enable GuardDuty across the entire organization from the management account
aws guardduty create-detector --enable

# Designate an admin account
aws guardduty enable-organization-admin-account \
  --admin-account-id 123456789012  # security tooling account

# From the security account: auto-enable for all current and future members
aws guardduty update-organization-configuration \
  --detector-id <detector-id> \
  --auto-enable-organization-members ALL \
  --features '[{"Name":"S3_DATA_EVENTS","AutoEnable":"ALL"},{"Name":"EKS_AUDIT_LOGS","AutoEnable":"ALL"}]'
Shared Controls in the Well-Architected Security Pillar

AWS's Well-Architected Framework Security Pillar provides a structured review methodology that maps directly to shared control categories. Running a Well-Architected Review for security is an effective way to identify gaps in your customer-side shared control implementations.
SEC 1: How do you securely operate your workload?

Covers organizational security practices, including training (shared control), change management (shared control), and threat modeling. The review asks whether you have security ownership, a security baseline, and documented security requirements — all customer-responsibility answers.
SEC 6: How do you protect your compute resources?

Directly addresses the customer half of patch management: vulnerability management, patch automation, container image hardening, reducing the attack surface of compute resources. If your answer involves manual patching or no patching policy, this becomes a High-Risk Issue (HRI) in the Well-Architected Review output.
SEC 8: How do you detect and investigate security events?

Directly addresses the customer half of logging and monitoring: CloudTrail configuration, GuardDuty, Security Hub, alerting, and incident response. A workload with logging enabled but no alerting receives a partial credit — logs without alerts fulfill the letter but not the spirit of the control.
Partner-led Well-Architected Reviews:

AWS Well-Architected Partner Program firms offer free Well-Architected Reviews as a mechanism to identify gaps and prioritize remediation. The output is a ranked list of High-Risk Issues and Medium-Risk Issues with specific improvement plans. This is an effective way to get an outside-in assessment of your shared control implementation maturity.
Shared Controls in Practice: A Real-World Security Event
Consider what happens when AWS detects unusual network traffic patterns in their data center fabric — this scenario illustrates how shared controls interact in practice. AWS's
infrastructure monitoring

(their shared control) flags the anomaly and their internal Security Operations team investigates. However, the traffic originates from an EC2 instance in your account, which means the response now transitions to your shared control obligations.

This sequence — AWS detects at the infrastructure layer, you respond at the workload layer — is the fundamental rhythm of shared controls. Neither party can complete the response alone. AWS cannot terminate your instance (doing so would violate the principle of customer autonomy); you cannot see the physical network anomaly (you have no access to AWS fabric metrics). The shared control of incident response requires both parties to execute their respective runbooks simultaneously.
Phase 1: Detection (AWS-Owned)
AWS's monitoring systems continuously analyze traffic patterns, hardware telemetry, and service health across all regions. When a GuardDuty finding such as
UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/MaliciousIPCaller

is generated, AWS has already performed the infrastructure-level detection. The finding is delivered to your account via the GuardDuty service — this is the handoff point between AWS's shared control obligation and yours.
Phase 2: Investigation (Customer-Owned)
Upon receiving the GuardDuty finding, your team must investigate the affected resource. This requires the logging and monitoring shared controls to be in place:
VPC Flow Logs
must be enabled (your responsibility — AWS provides the service, but you must turn it on),
CloudTrail
must be active in all regions (AWS provides the API, you must enable it), and
S3 access logs
must be configured on any data stores the instance touched.

Without these in place, your investigation stalls immediately — you have a GuardDuty finding pointing at an instance, but no visibility into what that instance did. This is the most common shared control gap in real incident investigations: AWS delivered the detection, but the customer had not fulfilled their half of the logging shared control.

import boto3
import json

def investigate_guardduty_finding(finding_id: str, detector_id: str) -> dict:
    """Pull GuardDuty finding details and correlate with VPC Flow Logs."""
    gd = boto3.client('guardduty')
    ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')
    
    finding = gd.get_findings(
        DetectorId=detector_id,
        FindingIds=[finding_id]
    )['Findings'][0]
    
    instance_id = finding['Resource']['InstanceDetails']['InstanceId']
    network_interface = finding['Resource']['InstanceDetails']['NetworkInterfaces'][0]
    vpc_id = network_interface['VpcId']
    
    # Check if VPC Flow Logs are enabled — common gap
    flow_logs = ec2.describe_flow_logs(
        Filters=[{'Name': 'resource-id', 'Values': [vpc_id]}]
    )['FlowLogs']
    
    return {
        'instance_id': instance_id,
        'finding_type': finding['Type'],
        'severity': finding['Severity'],
        'flow_logs_enabled': len(flow_logs) > 0,
        'flow_log_destinations': [fl['LogDestination'] for fl in flow_logs]
    }

# Containment: isolate the instance via security group swap
def quarantine_instance(instance_id: str, quarantine_sg_id: str):
    ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')
    
    # Get current security groups
    instance = ec2.describe_instances(InstanceIds=[instance_id])
    current_sgs = [sg['GroupId'] for sg in
                   instance['Reservations'][0]['Instances'][0]['SecurityGroups']]
    
    # Replace with quarantine SG (deny all traffic)
    ec2.modify_instance_attribute(
        InstanceId=instance_id,
        Groups=[quarantine_sg_id]
    )
    
    return {'quarantined': instance_id, 'previous_sgs': current_sgs}
Phase 3: Recovery and Post-Incident Review
Recovery is also a shared control. AWS provides services like
AWS Backup
RDS automated backups
, and
EC2 AMI snapshots

— but you must have configured them in advance, tested the restore process, and defined the Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) that dictate which backup tier to use. A customer who never tested their backup restore has not fulfilled their half of the shared control for recovery.
Post-incident, both parties produce artifacts. AWS's
Personal Health Dashboard
records any infrastructure-level events; your
CloudTrail
logs record what API calls were made; your
GuardDuty findings archive

records the detection timeline. For compliance frameworks like PCI DSS, auditors expect evidence from both layers. Having only one layer's evidence may result in a finding gap even if the incident was fully contained.
Integrating Shared Controls into Your CI/CD Pipeline

Shared controls cannot only be applied manually — at the scale of modern AWS environments, they must be enforced automatically at every deployment. A pipeline that deploys infrastructure without checking shared control compliance is a pipeline that will eventually drift out of compliance.
The three key integration points are:
pre-deployment policy checks
(block deployments that would violate shared control obligations),
post-deployment drift detection
(catch configuration that has changed since last deployment), and
continuous compliance monitoring
(ongoing validation that running infrastructure still meets the shared control baseline).

# buildspec.yml — enforce shared controls in CodeBuild
version: 0.2
phases:
  install:
    commands:
      - pip install checkov==3.2.0 boto3
  pre_build:
    commands:
      # Check Terraform plans for shared control violations
      - terraform init -input=false
      - terraform plan -out=tfplan -input=false
      - terraform show -json tfplan > tfplan.json
      # Block if CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, or Config would be disabled
      - |
        python3 << 'EOF'
        import json, sys
        plan = json.load(open('tfplan.json'))
        changes = plan.get('resource_changes', [])
        violations = []
        for r in changes:
          rt = r.get('type', '')
          after = r.get('change', {}).get('after', {})
          if rt == 'aws_cloudtrail' and after.get('enable_logging') == False:
            violations.append(f"CloudTrail logging disabled: {r['address']}")
          if rt == 'aws_flow_log' and r['change']['actions'] == ['delete']:
            violations.append(f"VPC Flow Log deleted: {r['address']}")
          if rt == 'aws_config_configuration_recorder' and after.get('recording_group') is None:
            violations.append(f"Config recorder may be misconfigured: {r['address']}")
        if violations:
          print("SHARED CONTROL VIOLATIONS DETECTED:")
          for v in violations: print(f"  - {v}")
          sys.exit(1)
        print("Shared control pre-deployment check passed.")
        EOF
  build:
    commands:
      - terraform apply -input=false tfplan
  post_build:
    commands:
      # After deployment, verify shared controls are still active
      - aws cloudtrail get-trail-status --name management-trail --query 'IsLogging'
      - aws configservice describe-configuration-recorder-status
        --query 'ConfigurationRecordersStatus[0].recording'
Beyond pre-deployment checks, AWS
Config Conformance Packs

provide a managed way to continuously evaluate shared control compliance across your entire organization. AWS publishes pre-built conformance packs for standards like NIST 800-53, CIS Benchmarks, and PCI DSS — each pack maps dozens of Config rules to the specific shared controls those frameworks require. Enabling a conformance pack is itself fulfilling your side of the configuration management shared control.

# Deploy NIST 800-53 conformance pack organization-wide
# This enforces shared control baselines across all member accounts

aws configservice put-organization-conformance-pack \
  --organization-conformance-pack-name "SharedControlsNIST80053" \
  --template-s3-uri "s3://aws-conformance-packs-us-east-1/Operational-Best-Practices-for-NIST-800-53.yaml" \
  --delivery-s3-bucket "my-conformance-pack-results-$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text)" \
  --region us-east-1

# Check pack deployment status across all accounts
aws configservice describe-organization-conformance-pack-statuses \
  --organization-conformance-pack-names "SharedControlsNIST80053" \
  --query "OrganizationConformancePackStatuses[].{Account:ErrorCode,Status:Status}"

# Get summary of compliance by rule across the organization
aws configservice get-organization-conformance-pack-compliance-summary \
  --organization-conformance-pack-names "SharedControlsNIST80053" \
  --query "OrganizationConformancePackComplianceSummaries[].{Pack:OrganizationConformancePackName,Compliant:Status.CompliantRuleCount,NonCompliant:Status.NonCompliantRuleCount}"
Key Takeaways
Shared controls require action on both sides:

A shared control is not covered just because AWS does it for the infrastructure. Your application-layer implementation of the same control category is equally required. Both halves must be present for the control to be effective.
The four shared control categories are patch management, configuration management, awareness and training, and logging and monitoring:
Each has an AWS-scope implementation (documented in AWS Artifact reports) and a customer-scope implementation (your responsibility to build and document).
AWS Artifact provides AWS's half as downloadable evidence:

Don't document AWS's patch management or training controls yourself — download the SOC 2 Type II report and reference the relevant control sections. This is the correct and efficient approach for compliance audits.
Configuration drift is a security event:

Any change to a resource that diverges from your IaC definition bypasses your review and approval process. Treat AWS Config drift findings and CloudFormation Stack Drift findings as potential security incidents requiring investigation.
Logging without alerting is forensics, not security:

CloudTrail enabled without CloudWatch alarms or GuardDuty means you can investigate a breach after it happens but not prevent or detect it in real time. The monitoring half of the logging shared control requires active alerting, not just log retention.
